article_title: PostgreSQL Ships Quarterly Update for Versions 14 Through 18, Patching 11 Security Vulnerabilities and Over 60 Bugs
summary: Duplicate of the already-published PostgreSQL May 2026 security-release article (PR #1269), AND it carries two Rule 3 quote-mark violations — two release-notes paraphrases presented inside quote marks as if verbatim. Rejected on both grounds.

findings.details: Covers the identical May 14, 2026 PostgreSQL coordinated security release as the published article 2026-05/14-postgresql-ships-coordinated-security-release-fixing-11-cves-across-five-supported-versions (from PR #1269). All three of this submission's sources are a subset of #1269's five sources.

findings.message: Two paraphrases presented inside quote marks (Rule 3 violations)
findings.details: 1. The article writes that a fix makes the MERGE statement "correctly reports serialization failures in repeatable-read/serializable modes" — quote marks around text that does not appear verbatim in the 18.4 release notes. The release notes actually say: 'Correctly report a serialization failure when MERGE encounters a concurrently-updated tuple in repeatable-read or serializable mode.' The article's quoted version alters the verb ('reports' vs 'report'), pluralizes 'failures', and compresses 'repeatable-read or serializable mode' to 'repeatable-read/serializable modes'. 2. The article quotes a fix for a "race condition between checkpoint replay and multixact ID creations" — again quote marks around non-verbatim text. The release notes say: 'Fix race condition between WAL replay of checkpoints and multixact ID creations.' The article rewrote 'WAL replay of checkpoints' as 'checkpoint replay' while keeping the quote marks.

content_preview.title: PostgreSQL Ships Quarterly Update for Versions 14 Through 18, Patching 11 Security Vulnerabilities and Over 60 Bugs
content_preview.summary: The May 14 update across PostgreSQL 14-18 fixes 11 CVEs, including an integer-overflow memory flaw and SQL injection in replication tools.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The PostgreSQL Global Development Group released a coordinated update to every supported version of the database on May 14, 2026, shipping PostgreSQL 18.4, 17.10, 16.14, 15.18, and 14.23. According to the [PostgreSQL Global Development Group](https://www.postgresql.org/about/news/postgresql-184-1710-1614-1518-and-1423-released-3297/), the release "fixes 11 security vulnerabilities and over 60 bugs reported over the last several months." It is the project's regular second-quarter mai...

recommendations: No rewrite — the PostgreSQL May 2026 security release is already covered by the published article 2026-05/14-postgresql-ships-coordinated-security-release-fixing-11-cves-across-five-supported-versions.

editor_notes.content_quality: The article is reasonably written and structured (798 words, News), but it is the weaker of the two colliding PostgreSQL submissions: it covers only about seven of the eleven CVEs and explicitly states it cannot provide CVSS severity scores, where the published sibling #1269 enumerates all eleven CVEs with scores and per-version ranges.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 3 source snapshots present and fetched at HTTP 200 (PostgreSQL announcement, PostgreSQL 18.4 release notes, Linux Compatible). All three are a subset of the five sources used by the published sibling article #1269. Most quotes verified verbatim against the release notes — 'assorted integer overflows in memory-allocation calculations', 'would lead to allocating a too-small buffer which the caller would then write past the end of', 'SQL injection could be achieved', 'allowing execution of arbitrary SQL on the publisher', 'unbounded recursion while processing startup packets', 'failed to validate output file paths read from their input' all appear verbatim, as do the announcement and Linux Compatible quotes. However, two quoted passages in the non-security bug-fix paragraph do NOT appear verbatim in the release notes (see Quote Integrity finding).
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: The underlying facts are accurate. The problem is not invented facts but two real facts wrapped in inexact 'quotes' — and, separately, that the whole article is redundant with already-published coverage.
editor_notes.originality: FAILS. This submission covers the identical event — the May 14, 2026 PostgreSQL coordinated security release across versions 14-18 — as the article published from PR #1269. Same release, same date, same '11 CVEs / 60+ bugs' framing, same EOL-November-12 close. All three sources here are a subset of #1269's sources. #1269 was selected as the stronger piece because it additionally cites the PostgreSQL security information page, enabling a complete CVE/CVSS/version enumeration this submission explicitly lacks.
editor_notes.quote_audit: Rule 3 check FAILED. Two paraphrases are presented inside quote marks as if verbatim: (1) 'correctly reports serialization failures in repeatable-read/serializable modes' — the release notes say 'Correctly report a serialization failure when MERGE encounters a concurrently-updated tuple in repeatable-read or serializable mode'; (2) 'race condition between checkpoint replay and multixact ID creations' — the release notes say 'Fix race condition between WAL replay of checkpoints and multixact ID creations'. This is the contributor bot's known pattern of paraphrasing inside quote marks. Both paraphrases are factually faithful to the source, but the quote marks misrepresent them as exact source text.
concerns: Duplicate of an already-published article covering the identical PostgreSQL security release.
Two Rule 3 quote-mark violations: release-notes paraphrases dressed as verbatim quotes.
recommendations: No rewrite. The PostgreSQL May 2026 security release is already covered by the published, stronger article from PR #1269. Even absent the duplication, the two paraphrase-inside-quote-marks issues would each need correcting; combined with the redundancy, REJECT is the correct verdict under v3.9.0.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: REJECT. Two independent grounds: (1) it duplicates the already-published #1269 coverage of the same release, and (2) it contains two Rule 3 quote-mark violations. A duplicate article that also misrepresents paraphrases as verbatim quotes is not eligible for APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS — the redundancy alone is decisive, and the quote issues confirm it.
